step_by_step_thinking:
For the statement "There is at least one tRNA for each of the 2 amino acids," we need to consider whether there's enough diversity in tRNAs to cover all possible combinations of amino acids. Given that there are over 20 standard amino acids involved in protein synthesis, having only two types of tRNAs would be insufficient. Therefore, the correct number must include many more than just two types of tRNAs per amino acid. Thus, the statement is false.
\boxed{D} Based on our review of the available information and current knowledge of molecular biology:
Each amino acid typically requires a unique set of tRNAs due to their distinct codons and corresponding anticodon sequences. Since there are approximately 20 common amino acids involved in proteins, there needs to be significantly more than just two different tRNAs assigned to any given amino acid. Hence, the claim stating "there is at least one tRNA for each of the 2 amino acids" is incorrect.
